title: CollidersAndNon
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $\pi$ a walk in $G$: \[ \pi =\lp v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp. \] A node $v_k$, or more precisely, the position $k \in \lC 0,\dots, n \rC$, on the walk $\pi$ is called:

  1. a non-collider on $\pi$, if there is at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$, i.e.\ if it falls into one of the following cases: \[\begin{array}{rccl} \text{ end-node: } & k \in \lC 0,n \rC, \\ \text{ left chain: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}, \\ \text{ right chain: } & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1},\\ \text{ fork: } & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}; \end{array}\]
  2. a collider on $\pi$, if it is of the form: \[v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1},\] i.e.\ if there are two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk $\pi$.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_15 -- "CollidersAndNon".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_15
% title: CollidersAndNon
\def\rowref{def\_3\_15}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_15}

\begin{Def}[Colliders and non-colliders]\label{def:collider_noncollider}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and the edge classifications of def \ref{def-edge-relations}. Let
    \[
        \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
    \]
    be a walk in $G$ (def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.) for some integer $n \ge 0$, with $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$ satisfying the walk constraint at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$:
    \[
        \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr).
    \]

    \emph{Arrowhead count at a position.}
    For a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the \emph{arrowhead count at $v_k$ on $\pi$}, denoted $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$, is the cardinality
    \[
        \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)
            := \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|,
    \]
    where ``$a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$'' is in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.; spelled out, for any $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$ writing $a_i = (e_1, e_2)$,
    \[
        a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k
        \quad\iff\quad
        \bigl(a_i \in E \;\land\; e_2 = v_k\bigr)
            \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_i \in L \;\land\; (e_1 = v_k \,\lor\, e_2 = v_k)\bigr).
    \]
    The index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ ranges only over the (at most two) walk-incident edges at position $k$, namely $a_{k-1}$ (when $k \ge 1$) and $a_k$ (when $k \le n - 1$); edges of $G$ that are not incident to $v_k$ along $\pi$ are excluded by construction. At an end-position $k \in \{0, n\}$ at most one such index is admissible, so $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ automatically (and $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(0) = 0$ on the trivial walk $n = 0$, where neither index is admissible). At an interior position $1 \le k \le n - 1$ both indices are admissible, so $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \in \{0, 1, 2\}$.

    \emph{Classification.}
    The position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on the walk $\pi$ -- or, by abuse, the node $v_k$ at position $k$ -- is called:
    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item a \emph{non-collider on $\pi$} iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$;
        \item a \emph{collider on $\pi$} iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$.
    \end{enumerate}
    By construction these two classifications are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive over $\{0, 1, \dots, n\}$: every position $k$ on $\pi$ is exactly one of a non-collider or a collider on $\pi$.

    \emph{Reconciliation with the source-block pattern writings.}
    The source block characterises non-colliders by a list of four pattern-cases (end-node, left chain, right chain, fork) glossed as ``at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$'', and colliders by a single pattern-case glossed as ``two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk $\pi$''. Below we reconcile these visual writings with the count-based classification, taking the count-based classification (the LN's ``i.e.''\ gloss) as canonical. The visual writings $v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$, $v_{k-1} \huh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ are unfolded per def \ref{not-cdmg}, items~2--6.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{end-node} ($k \in \{0, n\}$): $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ holds automatically, hence the position is a non-collider on $\pi$. (For the trivial walk $n = 0$, the unique position $k = 0 = n$ has $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(0) = 0$ and is a non-collider.)
        \item \textbf{left chain} ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$): the walk's $a_{k-1}$ is \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$, while its $a_k$ \emph{is} an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out via the walk constraint, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ (a directed edge with arrowhead at $v_{k-1}$ and tail at $v_k$), and either $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$. Count: $0 + 1 = 1$; non-collider.
        \item \textbf{right chain} ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$): the walk's $a_{k-1}$ \emph{is} an edge into $v_k$, while its $a_k$ is \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out, either $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$, and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Count: $1 + 0 = 1$; non-collider.
        \item \textbf{fork} ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$): neither walk-incident edge is an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Count: $0 + 0 = 0$; non-collider.
        \item \textbf{collider} ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$): both walk-incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$ are edges into $v_k$. Spelled out, $a_{k-1} \in E \cup L$ with right-component $v_k$ if directed (or either component $v_k$ if bidirected), and analogously for $a_k$ with left-component $v_k$ if directed (or either component $v_k$ if bidirected). Count: $1 + 1 = 2$; collider.
    \end{itemize}
    The four non-collider patterns realise exactly the interior counts $\{0, 1\}$, and the collider pattern realises exactly the interior count $2$; together with the end-node case they exhaust the positions of $\pi$. The pattern-cases consult \emph{walk-incident} edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$ only -- arrowheads contributed by edges of $G$ that are not on $\pi$ at $v_k$ are excluded from $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ by construction. (This fixes the scope of the LN's gloss ``at most one (resp.\ two) arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$''.)

    \emph{Treatment of directed self-loops.}
    A directed self-loop $(v, v) \in E$ is admitted by def \ref{def-cdmg} (the irreflexivity restriction applies to $L$, not to $E$). If a walk-incident edge $a_i$ happens to be such a self-loop -- so $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) = (v, v) \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1} = v$, and we are evaluating $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ at a position $k \in \{i, i + 1\}$ for which $v_k = v$ -- then $a_i$ \emph{is} an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.\ (the $E$-clause fires via $e_2 = v = v_k$), so $a_i$ contributes $1$ to $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$. The count-based classification above then assigns a single, well-defined collider/non-collider status to $k$ on $\pi$ regardless of self-loop traversals.

    The visual pattern-match writings of the source block do \emph{not} share this property at directed self-loops: the conditions ``$v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$'' and ``$v_{k-1} \hut v_k$'' (def \ref{not-cdmg}, items~2--3) unfold to $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ and $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ respectively, which coincide as the single condition $(v, v) \in E$ when $v_{k-1} = v_k = v$, so a single interior position $k$ traversing a directed self-loop can simultaneously satisfy the ``left chain'', ``right chain'', ``fork'', and ``collider'' pattern conjunctions. The count-based classification above is taken as the canonical reading and uniformly resolves this apparent overlap: at such a $k$ the classification is whichever of non-collider / collider matches $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ under the ``edge into $v_k$'' count of the walk-incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the edge classifications of def def-edge-relations. Let \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] be a walk in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.) for some integer $n \ge 0$, with $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$ satisfying the walk constraint at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$: \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr). \]

Arrowhead count at a position. For a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the arrowhead count at $v_k$ on $\pi$, denoted $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$, is the cardinality \[ \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) := \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|, \] where “$a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ in $G$” is in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; spelled out, for any $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$ writing $a_i = (e_1, e_2)$, \[ a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \quad\iff\quad \bigl(a_i \in E \;\land\; e_2 = v_k\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_i \in L \;\land\; (e_1 = v_k \,\lor\, e_2 = v_k)\bigr). \] The index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ ranges only over the (at most two) walk-incident edges at position $k$, namely $a_{k-1}$ (when $k \ge 1$) and $a_k$ (when $k \le n - 1$); edges of $G$ that are not incident to $v_k$ along $\pi$ are excluded by construction. At an end-position $k \in \{0, n\}$ at most one such index is admissible, so $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ automatically (and $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(0) = 0$ on the trivial walk $n = 0$, where neither index is admissible). At an interior position $1 \le k \le n - 1$ both indices are admissible, so $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \in \{0, 1, 2\}$.

Classification. The position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ on the walk $\pi$ – or, by abuse, the node $v_k$ at position $k$ – is called:

  1. a non-collider on $\pi$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$;
  2. a collider on $\pi$ iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$.

By construction these two classifications are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive over $\{0, 1, \dots, n\}$: every position $k$ on $\pi$ is exactly one of a non-collider or a collider on $\pi$.

Reconciliation with the source-block pattern writings. The source block characterises non-colliders by a list of four pattern-cases (end-node, left chain, right chain, fork) glossed as “at most one arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$”, and colliders by a single pattern-case glossed as “two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk $\pi$”. Below we reconcile these visual writings with the count-based classification, taking the count-based classification (the LN's “i.e.”\ gloss) as canonical. The visual writings $v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$, $v_{k-1} \huh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$, $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ are unfolded per def not-cdmg, items 2–6.

  • end-node ($k \in \{0, n\}$): $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ holds automatically, hence the position is a non-collider on $\pi$. (For the trivial walk $n = 0$, the unique position $k = 0 = n$ has $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(0) = 0$ and is a non-collider.)
  • left chain ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$): the walk's $a_{k-1}$ is not an edge into $v_k$, while its $a_k$ is an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out via the walk constraint, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ (a directed edge with arrowhead at $v_{k-1}$ and tail at $v_k$), and either $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$. Count: $0 + 1 = 1$; non-collider.
  • right chain ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$): the walk's $a_{k-1}$ is an edge into $v_k$, while its $a_k$ is not an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out, either $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ or $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$, and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Count: $1 + 0 = 1$; non-collider.
  • fork ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$): neither walk-incident edge is an edge into $v_k$. Spelled out, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$. Count: $0 + 0 = 0$; non-collider.
  • collider ($1 \le k \le n - 1$; LN writing $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$): both walk-incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$ are edges into $v_k$. Spelled out, $a_{k-1} \in E \cup L$ with right-component $v_k$ if directed (or either component $v_k$ if bidirected), and analogously for $a_k$ with left-component $v_k$ if directed (or either component $v_k$ if bidirected). Count: $1 + 1 = 2$; collider.

The four non-collider patterns realise exactly the interior counts $\{0, 1\}$, and the collider pattern realises exactly the interior count $2$; together with the end-node case they exhaust the positions of $\pi$. The pattern-cases consult walk-incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$ only – arrowheads contributed by edges of $G$ that are not on $\pi$ at $v_k$ are excluded from $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ by construction. (This fixes the scope of the LN's gloss “at most one (resp.\ two) arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$”.)

Treatment of directed self-loops. A directed self-loop $(v, v) \in E$ is admitted by def def-cdmg (the irreflexivity restriction applies to $L$, not to $E$). If a walk-incident edge $a_i$ happens to be such a self-loop – so $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) = (v, v) \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1} = v$, and we are evaluating $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ at a position $k \in \{i, i + 1\}$ for which $v_k = v$ – then $a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.\ (the $E$-clause fires via $e_2 = v = v_k$), so $a_i$ contributes $1$ to $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$. The count-based classification above then assigns a single, well-defined collider/non-collider status to $k$ on $\pi$ regardless of self-loop traversals.

The visual pattern-match writings of the source block do not share this property at directed self-loops: the conditions “$v_{k-1} \tuh v_k$” and “$v_{k-1} \hut v_k$” (def not-cdmg, items 2–3) unfold to $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ and $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ respectively, which coincide as the single condition $(v, v) \in E$ when $v_{k-1} = v_k = v$, so a single interior position $k$ traversing a directed self-loop can simultaneously satisfy the “left chain”, “right chain”, “fork”, and “collider” pattern conjunctions. The count-based classification above is taken as the canonical reading and uniformly resolves this apparent overlap: at such a $k$ the classification is whichever of non-collider / collider matches $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ under the “edge into $v_k$” count of the walk-incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `IsCollider` and `IsNonCollider`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the ambient `CDMG Node` carrier: the structure stores `J, V, E, L` as `Finset`-valued fields, and `Finset` needs decidable equality on the element type for membership, union, and filter. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. The same `variable` line wraps every prior chapter-3 file (`CDMG.lean`, `CDMGNotation.lean`, `EdgeRelations.lean`, `Walks.lean`), keeping the section-3.3 signatures continuous with the section-3.1 chapter convention.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability is required by every `Finset` field of `CDMG Node`.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This second `variable` line sits inside `namespace Walk` and auto-binds the ambient CDMG `G : CDMG Node` as an implicit argument to every subsequent declaration in the namespace. It is the Lean encoding of the LN's once-at-the-top quantifier ``let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG'', threaded through `IsCollider` and `IsNonCollider` without each signature having to repeat `{G : CDMG Node}` explicitly. The binder is implicit (`{G}` rather than `(G)`) because downstream consumers always reach into `G` via dot-notation on a walk — `p.IsCollider k` rather than `Walk.IsCollider G p k` — so the CDMG is recovered from the walk's type `Walk G u v` by unification at the use site. Pairing this with the first helper, the chapter's standard prelude is in place: `Node` provides the abstract vertex carrier with decidable equality, and `G` fixes the ambient graph against which positions on walks are classified.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Namespace-wide implicit `G`; recovered by unification from each walk's `Walk G u v` type, no explicit binder on the defs.
variable {G : CDMG Node}

The binder is implicit (`{G}` rather than `(G)`) because downstream consumers always reach into `G` via dot-notation on a walk — `p.IsCollider k` rather than `Walk.IsCollider G p k` — so the CDMG is recovered from the walk's type `Walk G u v` by unification at the use site. lean_blocks.code:
def IsInto : ∀ {u v : Node}, WalkStep G u v → Node → Prop
  | u, v, .forwardE _,  w => w = v ∨ (s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ (w = u ∨ w = v))
  | u, v, .backwardE _, w => w = u ∨ (s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ (w = u ∨ w = v))
  | u, v, .bidir _,     w => w = u ∨ w = v
lean_blocks.explanation: `WalkStep.IsInto s w` is the typed-WalkStep transcription of `def_3_3` item ii's ``edge into a node'' primitive: it asks whether the step `s : WalkStep G u v` places an arrowhead at the node `w` when traversed. Under the refactor a `WalkStep` carries its channel as a constructor tag (`.forwardE`, `.backwardE`, `.bidir`) and its endpoints as Type-level indices `u v`, so the per-step classification can be read off by pattern-matching rather than going through the original `G.into v (a : Node × Node)` membership-disjunction. The naming follows the LN's ``edge into $v_k$'' phrasing: `IsInto vk` reads ``has its arrowhead at `vk`''. Three subtleties are absorbed here: (1) tests use node-equality on type indices, so a directed self-loop with `u = v` correctly fires `IsInto v = True`; (2) the writing-mirror union disjunct `s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ …` on `.forwardE` / `.backwardE` preserves constructor-choice invariance when the stored pair simultaneously lives in `G.L`; (3) `.bidir` needs no symmetric E-disjunct because its L-clause already places arrowheads at both endpoints. The sole consumer is `Walk.IsCollider` below.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `IsInto s w`: does WalkStep `s` place an arrowhead at node `w`? Per-channel pattern match.
def IsInto : ∀ {u v : Node}, WalkStep G u v → Node → Prop
  -- Forward directed edge `u → v`: arrowhead at target `v`; writing-mirror disjunct re-injects L-channel if pair also in `G.L`.
  | u, v, .forwardE _,  w => w = v ∨ (s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ (w = u ∨ w = v))
  -- Backward directed edge `v → u`: arrowhead at source-index `u` (target of underlying directed edge); same writing-mirror disjunct.
  | u, v, .backwardE _, w => w = u ∨ (s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ (w = u ∨ w = v))
  -- Bidirected edge `s(u, v) ∈ G.L`: arrowheads at BOTH endpoints; no extra E-disjunct needed (L-clause dominates).
  | u, v, .bidir _,     w => w = u ∨ w = v

The binder is implicit (`{G}` rather than `(G)`) because downstream consumers always reach into `G` via dot-notation on a walk — `p.IsCollider k` rather than `Walk.IsCollider G p k` — so the CDMG is recovered from the walk's type `Walk G u v` by unification at the use site. Pairing this with the first helper, the chapter's standard prelude is in place: `Node` provides the abstract vertex carrier with decidable equality, and `G` fixes the ambient graph against which positions on walks are classified.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Namespace-wide implicit `G`; recovered by unification from each walk's `Walk G u v` type, no explicit binder on the defs.
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.code:
def IsCollider : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (.nil _ _), _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  | _, _, .cons vk s₀ (.cons _ s₁ _), 1 =>
      s₀.IsInto vk ∧ s₁.IsInto vk
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)), k + 2 => p.IsCollider (k + 1)
lean_blocks.explanation: `Walk.IsCollider p k` is the position-level collider predicate: position `k` on `p` is a collider iff its arrowhead count $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$, i.e. both walk-incident steps `s_{k-1}` and `s_k` exist and both place an arrowhead at the position-`k` vertex `v_k`. Under the typed-WalkStep refactor `p.edges` no longer exists, so the original's Option-membership encoding is replaced by a structural recursion on the `Walk` constructors. Five branches: `nil` and `cons-nil` (length-0 and length-1 walks have no interior positions) return `False` for every `k`; `cons-cons` at `k = 0` returns `False` (position 0 is always an end-position); `cons-cons` at `k = 1` is the load-bearing branch that binds the middle vertex `vk` and the two head steps `s₀, s₁` and tests `s₀.IsInto vk ∧ s₁.IsInto vk`, mirroring the LN's ``two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$''; `cons-cons` at `k + 2` recurses into the tail at `k + 1` (the index shift absorbs the one-cons-cell descent). End-position and out-of-range cases bottom out at the `nil` / `cons-nil` branches uniformly. The `IsInto` helper absorbs the self-loop and writing-mirror conventions, so this predicate's body stays clean.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `IsCollider p k`: position `k` on walk `p` is a collider (both walk-incident steps point arrowheads at `v_k`).
def IsCollider : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  -- Trivial walk (length 0): no interior positions, every `k` returns `False`.
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  -- Length-1 walk: only end-positions `k ∈ {0, 1}`, no interior, every `k` returns `False`.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (.nil _ _), _ => False
  -- Length ≥ 2 walk at position 0: position 0 is always an end-position, never a collider.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (.cons _ _ _), 0 => False
  -- Length ≥ 2 walk at position 1: bind middle vertex `vk = v_1` and head steps `s₀, s₁`; collider iff both arrow into `vk`.
  | _, _, .cons vk s₀ (.cons _ s₁ _), 1 =>
      s₀.IsInto vk ∧ s₁.IsInto vk
  -- Position `k + 2`: descend one cons-cell into the tail `p`; outer position `k + 2` becomes tail position `k + 1`.
  | _, _, .cons _ _ (p@(.cons _ _ _)), k + 2 => p.IsCollider (k + 1)

lean_blocks.code:
def IsNonCollider {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
  k ≤ p.length ∧ ¬ p.IsCollider k
lean_blocks.explanation: This block defines the position-level non-collider predicate `Walk.IsNonCollider p k` as the negation of `IsCollider` on the in-range fragment $k \in \{0, \dots, p.length\}$. Since the arrowhead count $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ lives in $\{0, 1, 2\}$, the LN classification ``$\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$'' is equivalent to ``$\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \neq 2$'', i.e.\ to `¬ p.IsCollider k`, which makes the LN's mutual-exclusivity statement (every in-range position is exactly one of collider / non-collider) definitional rather than a separate theorem. The explicit bound `k ≤ p.length` is load-bearing: without it, every out-of-range `k > p.length` would vacuously satisfy `¬ p.IsCollider k` (since `IsCollider` is `False` off-range through the Option-`some` conjuncts) and would be misclassified. The asymmetry with the sibling block — `IsCollider`'s in-range condition is implicit via Option-membership, while `IsNonCollider` carries it explicitly — is the cost of the negation-based encoding; the payoff is that the two predicates are pointwise complementary on $\{0, \dots, p.length\}$ by unfolding alone.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Non-collider at position `k`: in-range AND not a collider; mutual exclusivity with `IsCollider` becomes definitional.
def IsNonCollider {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
  -- Explicit in-range bound; `IsCollider`'s implicit Option-membership scoping is not available on the negation side.
  k ≤ p.length ∧ ¬ p.IsCollider k

design_choices:
**Per-step `WalkStep.IsInto` helper, not inline pattern enumeration.** The refactor's typed `WalkStep` carries its channel as a constructor tag (`.forwardE`, `.backwardE`, `.bidir`) and its endpoints as Type-level indices. A naive inline reading at `IsCollider`'s `k = 1` branch would have to enumerate all nine combinations of head-tag × tail-tag and ask ``does this pair encode arrowheads into $v_k$ from both sides?'' Factoring ``this step contributes an arrowhead at node `w`'' out as `IsInto` lets the consumer mirror the LN's phrasing word-for-word as the conjunction `s₀.IsInto vk ∧ s₁.IsInto vk` — one conjunct per ``arrowhead pointing towards $v_k$'' — and isolates the load-bearing self-loop and writing-mirror corner cases into a single primitive.

**`IsInto` tests by node-equality on type indices, not by constructor-tag ``source vs target''.** A rigid tag reading would label `.forwardE _ : WalkStep G v v` (a directed self-loop at `v`) as ``arrowhead at target but not at source'' — a semantically empty statement that cannot distinguish ``arrowhead at the loop vertex'' from ``no arrowhead at the loop vertex'' and that would diverge from the original's `G.into v (v, v) = True`. The node-equality test `w = v` (resp. `w = u`) on type indices fires correctly at self-loops where the source and target coincide, preserving the canonical tex's count-based reading verbatim.

**Writing-mirror union disjunct on `.forwardE` / `.backwardE`, none on `.bidir`.** `def_3_1` does not enforce $E \cap L = \emptyset$: the same vertex pair may simultaneously support an L-edge and a directed edge, and the walker may then legally encode the step as either `.forwardE h_E` or `.bidir h_L`. The LN's ``edge into $v_k$'' is a *union* over the stored pair's two channels, so the typed-WalkStep predicate must restore that union to remain constructor-choice-invariant. The disjunct `s(u, v) ∈ G.L ∧ (w = u ∨ w = v)` re-injects the L-clause whenever the stored directed pair also lives in `G.L`. No symmetric E-disjunct is needed on `.bidir` because its L-clause already places arrowheads at *both* endpoints, dominating anything an E-disjunct could contribute ($P \lor Q \iff P$ whenever $Q \to P$). At directed self-loops the L-disjunct is vacuously false via `hL_irrefl`, so the self-loop and writing-mirror fixes coexist by typing rather than by special-casing.

**Structural recursion on `Walk` constructors, no `p.edges`-style indexing.** The refactor intentionally drops `Walk.edges` (see `Walks.lean`'s ``Why no `edges`'' block), so the original's `p.edges[k]?` Option-membership idiom has no port. `IsCollider` recurses on the `nil` / `cons-nil` / `cons-cons` constructor shape instead: trivial and length-1 walks (no interior positions) return `False` for every `k`; the `k = 0` branch on length-≥-2 walks returns `False`; the `k = 1` branch is the dedicated arrowhead test on the two head steps; the `k + 2` branch descends one cons-cell into the tail with the index shifted to `k + 1`. End-position and out-of-range cases bottom out structurally at the `False` branches, with no explicit `1 ≤ k` or `k ≤ p.length - 1` guard needed.

**Dedicated position-1 branch, recursion only from position `k + 2`.** Position 1 is special-cased because testing collider-ness there requires reading both *head* steps `s_0` and `s_1` simultaneously, both bound at the outer cons-cons pattern. Recursing into the tail at position 1 would drop `s_0` (which lives at the outer head, not in the tail) and lose the LHS of the conjunction. The recursion's first opportunity is therefore outer position `k + 2`, where the relevant pair is the tail's first two steps. A reader tempted to unify the position-1 and position-`k + 2` branches would have to either carry `s_0` through a companion helper or reach into the tail's cons — both strictly more complex than the dedicated branch.

**`IsCollider` primary, `IsNonCollider` as bounded negation.** Encoding `IsNonCollider` as `k ≤ p.length ∧ ¬ p.IsCollider k` makes the LN's mutual-exclusivity statement (``every position is exactly one of collider / non-collider'') definitional on the in-range fragment rather than a separate theorem. The asymmetry — `IsCollider`'s in-range condition is implicit in its recursive structure, while `IsNonCollider` carries `k ≤ p.length` explicitly because $\lnot$ `IsCollider` is vacuously true off-range — is the price for this definitional shape. The alternative, defining both predicates positively via a shared $\mathbb{N}$-valued count helper, would require a `Decidable (G.into v e)` instance to evaluate the count and an external proof that $\mathrm{count} \le 1 \iff \mathrm{count} \ne 2$.

**`Prop`, not `Bool`.** Walks live at `Type _`; their position-level classification at `Prop`. A decidable `Bool` form would force a `DecidableEq Node` discharge at every elaboration site for no downstream benefit — `def_3_16` blockability and `def_3_17` $\sigma$-blocking consume these predicates as hypotheses, where `Prop` is the natural fit.

**Distinct from `Walk.IsColliderWalk` (def_3_4 item iv).** `IsColliderWalk` is a *whole-walk* shape predicate that additionally constrains endpoints and handles the $n = 1$ case via a bidirected-edge constraint; the present `IsCollider` is *position-level*. The names diverge intentionally so that pointwise consumers downstream do not silently inherit walk-level constraints — even on the interior fragment $1 \le k \le n - 1$, the conjunction $\forall k, \, p.\mathrm{IsCollider}\, k$ is strictly weaker than `p.IsColliderWalk`.
